questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The speed of light is dependent
options: exclusively on the inertia of the medium
exclusively on the elasticity of the medium
questions.solution:
Light is an electromagnetic wave and does not require any material medium for its propagation.

The propagation of mechanical waves depends on the properties of the medium such as elasticity and inertia.

Since light is a non-mechanical wave, its speed does not depend on the elasticity and inertia of the medium.

The speed of light in a vacuum is given by $c = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}$ and in a medium by $v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \epsilon}}$, where $\mu$ and $\epsilon$ are the magnetic permeability and electric permittivity of the medium.

Therefore, the speed of light depends on neither elasticity nor inertia.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following phenomena provides evidence that light is a transverse wave?
questions.solution:
Interference, diffraction, and reflection are phenomena exhibited by both transverse and longitudinal waves.

Polarization is a property unique to transverse waves, where the oscillations are restricted to a particular direction perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation.

Since light can be polarized, it provides conclusive evidence that light is a transverse wave.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As light undergoes refraction into a medium,
options: its wavelength decreases while its frequency remains unchanged
its wavelength increases while its frequency remains unchanged
both its wavelength and frequency increase
both its wavelength and frequency decrease
questions.solution:
When light travels from vacuum or air into an optically denser medium of refractive index $n$, its speed decreases according to the relation $v = \dfrac{c}{n}$.

The frequency $f$ of light is a fundamental property of the source and remains unchanged during refraction across any boundary.

Using the wave equation $v = f \lambda$, since the speed $v$ decreases and the frequency $f$ remains constant, the wavelength $\lambda$ must decrease ($\lambda' = \dfrac{\lambda}{n}$).

Therefore, its wavelength decreases while its frequency remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light is best described as a
questions.solution: Light exhibits wave-particle duality. In phenomena such as interference, diffraction, and polarization, light behaves as a wave. In phenomena such as the photoelectric effect and Compton scattering, light behaves as a particle (photon). Therefore, light is best described as both a particle and a wave phenomenon.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light wave is described by the equation $y = A \sin(kx - \omega t)$. In this expression, $y$ denotes the
questions.solution:
Light is an electromagnetic wave consisting of oscillating electric and magnetic fields that propagate through space.

The given equation $y = A \sin(kx - \omega t)$ represents the propagation of a light wave, where $y$ denotes the instantaneous value of the oscillating electric field (or magnetic field).

Pressure and density variations are characteristics of longitudinal mechanical waves, such as sound waves.

The concept of ether particles is obsolete as electromagnetic waves do not require a material medium for propagation.

Therefore, $y$ represents the electric field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following characteristics remains unchanged when light undergoes refraction?
questions.solution:
When light undergoes refraction, it travels from one medium to another. The frequency of light depends only on the source of the light and not on the medium through which it travels. Therefore, frequency remains unchanged.

The velocity of light changes according to the refractive index of the medium, given by $v = \dfrac{c}{n}$.

Since $v = f \lambda$ and frequency $f$ is constant, the wavelength $\lambda$ also changes when velocity changes.

The amplitude of light generally decreases during refraction because a portion of the incident light is reflected at the boundary of the two media, reducing the energy and intensity of the refracted wave.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An amplitude modulated (AM) radio wave bends noticeably around the corners of a $1 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m}$ board, whereas a frequency modulated (FM) wave bends only negligibly. Given that the average wavelengths for the AM and FM waves are $\lambda_a$ and $\lambda_f$ respectively, which of the following is true?
options: There is not enough information to determine the relationship between $\lambda_a$ and $\lambda_f$.
questions.solution:
For diffraction (bending of waves around corners) to be noticeable, the wavelength of the wave must be comparable to or greater than the size of the obstacle.

Since the AM wave bends noticeably around the $1 \text{ m} \times 1 \text{ m}$ board, its wavelength $\lambda_a$ must be comparable to or greater than $1 \text{ m}$.

Since the FM wave bends negligibly, its wavelength $\lambda_f$ must be smaller than the dimensions of the board.

Therefore, $\lambda_a > \lambda_f$.

This is also consistent with typical radio frequencies, where AM operates around $1 \text{ MHz}$ ($\lambda_a \approx 300 \text{ m}$) and FM operates around $100 \text{ MHz}$ ($\lambda_f \approx 3 \text{ m}$).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the sources listed below produces the best monochromatic light?
questions.solution:
A laser produces light that is highly coherent, directional, and monochromatic, meaning it consists of a single wavelength or color.

A bulb and a candle emit light that contains a continuous spectrum of many wavelengths, making them polychromatic sources.

A mercury tube emits light consisting of several discrete wavelengths, so it is not strictly monochromatic.

Therefore, a laser is the best source of monochromatic light among the given options.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The wavefronts of a light wave travelling in vacuum are defined by $x + y + z = c$. The angle formed by the direction of propagation of the light with the X-axis is
questions.solution:
The equation of the wavefront is given by $x + y + z = c$.

The direction of propagation of a light wave is always perpendicular to its wavefront.

The normal vector to the plane $x + y + z = c$ represents the direction of propagation, which is $\vec{n} = \hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k}$.

The angle $\alpha$ made by the direction of propagation with the X-axis (direction vector $\hat{i}$) is given by

$\cos \alpha = \dfrac{\vec{n} \cdot \hat{i}}{|\vec{n}| |\hat{i}|}$

$\cos \alpha = \dfrac{(\hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k}) \cdot \hat{i}}{\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2} \sqrt{1^2}}$

$\cos \alpha = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

$\alpha = \cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The wavefronts of light arriving from a distant source of unknown shape are approximately
questions.solution:
When a light wave travels a very large distance from its source, the radius of curvature of the wavefront becomes extremely large.

As a result, a small portion of the wavefront incident on an observer or an optical instrument appears to be flat.

Thus, the wavefronts of light arriving from a distant source are approximately plane wavefronts, regardless of the initial shape of the source.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The inverse square law of intensity (where intensity $\propto \dfrac{1}{r^2}$) holds true for a
questions.solution:
For a point source, the power $P$ is distributed uniformly over a spherical surface of radius $r$.

The surface area of the sphere is $A = 4\pi r^2$.

Intensity $I = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{P}{4\pi r^2}$.

Thus, $I \propto \dfrac{1}{r^2}$, which is the inverse square law.

For a line or cylindrical source, the power is distributed over a cylindrical surface, so $I \propto \dfrac{1}{r}$.

For a plane source, the intensity is independent of distance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two sources are considered coherent if the waves they produce
questions.solution:
Two sources are defined as coherent if the waves emitted by them have the same frequency and maintain a constant phase difference with respect to each other over time.

If the phase difference changes randomly with time, the sources are incoherent and cannot produce a stable interference pattern.

Thus, maintaining a constant phase difference is the essential condition for coherence.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coherent sources with different intensities emit waves that interfere with each other. If the ratio of the maximum intensity to the minimum intensity is $25$, the ratio of the intensities of the two sources is
questions.solution:
Let the intensities of the two coherent sources be $I_1$ and $I_2$.

The maximum intensity in the interference pattern is given by $I_{max} = (\sqrt{I_1} + \sqrt{I_2})^2$

The minimum intensity is given by $I_{min} = (\sqrt{I_1} - \sqrt{I_2})^2$

Given that the ratio of maximum to minimum intensity is $25$:

$\dfrac{I_{max}}{I_{min}} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{I_1} + \sqrt{I_2})^2}{(\sqrt{I_1} - \sqrt{I_2})^2} = 25$

Taking the square root on both sides:

$\dfrac{\sqrt{I_1} + \sqrt{I_2}}{\sqrt{I_1} - \sqrt{I_2}} = 5$

$\sqrt{I_1} + \sqrt{I_2} = 5\sqrt{I_1} - 5\sqrt{I_2}$

$6\sqrt{I_2} = 4\sqrt{I_1}$

$\dfrac{\sqrt{I_1}}{\sqrt{I_2}} = \dfrac{6}{4} = \dfrac{3}{2}$

Squaring both sides, the ratio of the intensities is:

$\dfrac{I_1}{I_2} = \dfrac{9}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a thin transparent sheet is placed in front of a Young's double slit, the fringe-width will
questions.solution:
The fringe width in Young's double slit experiment is given by $\beta = \dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$.

When a thin transparent sheet of thickness $t$ and refractive index $\mu$ is placed in front of one of the slits, an additional path difference of $(\mu - 1)t$ is introduced. This causes the entire fringe pattern to shift by a distance $\Delta y = \dfrac{D}{d}(\mu - 1)t$.

The fringe width $\beta$ depends only on the wavelength $\lambda$, the distance between the slits and the screen $D$, and the distance between the slits $d$. Since these parameters remain unchanged, the fringe width remains the same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When Young's double slit experiment is conducted in water,
options: the fringe width will remain unchanged
questions.solution:
The fringe width in Young's double slit experiment is given by $\beta = \dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$, where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light, $D$ is the distance between the slits and the screen, and $d$ is the distance between the slits.

When the experiment is conducted in water, the wavelength of light changes to $\lambda' = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$, where $\mu$ is the refractive index of water.

The new fringe width becomes $\beta' = \dfrac{\lambda' D}{d} = \dfrac{\lambda D}{\mu d} = \dfrac{\beta}{\mu}$.

Since the refractive index of water $\mu > 1$, the new fringe width $\beta'$ is less than the original fringe width $\beta$.

Therefore, the fringe width will decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a drop of oil spreads across a water surface, it exhibits beautiful colours in daylight due to
questions.solution:
When a drop of oil spreads across a water surface, it forms a thin film. When white light from the sun falls on this thin film, a portion of the light is reflected from the upper surface of the oil film and another portion is reflected from the lower surface (oil-water interface).

These two reflected light waves superimpose and interfere with each other. The condition for constructive or destructive interference depends on the thickness of the film and the wavelength of the light. Since white light consists of different wavelengths (colours), different colours undergo constructive interference at different thicknesses of the film.

This phenomenon of thin-film interference is responsible for the beautiful colours seen in the oil drop.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a Young's double slit experiment, the slits possess equal width, and the source is positioned symmetrically relative to them. The intensity at the central fringe is $I_0$. If one of the slits is subsequently blocked, the intensity at this point will become
questions.solution:
Let the intensity of light from each slit be $I$.

When both slits are open, the intensity at the central fringe is maximum because the path difference is zero.

The resultant intensity is given by $I_{0} = I + I + 2\sqrt{I \cdot I} \cos(0^{\circ}) = 4I$.

When one of the slits is blocked, there is no interference. The intensity at the central point is solely due to the light from the remaining single slit.

Thus, the new intensity is $I$.

From the relation $I_{0} = 4I$, we get $I = \dfrac{I_{0}}{4}$.
